region_of_origin: United States (popularised globally; served in Singapore by Potato Corner)

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn FOODON_00002694 covers french fries as a category. Waffle-cut fries are a shape variant; no dedicated waffle-fries term exists in FoodOn as of 2026-04.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A039V covers fried potato products broadly. No waffle-fries-specific facet exists; closest_parent applied.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept for waffle fries or seasoned waffle fries identified as of 2026-04.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of Mega Waffle Fries from Potato Corner is approximately 115 g, equivalent to a regular-sized box of seasoned waffle-cut fries.

nutrient_source.title: Potato, french fried, all types, salt added in processing, frozen, oven-heated — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 170697); adapted for seasoned deep-fried waffle fries
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: USDA SR Legacy entry for frozen oven-heated french fries (FDC ID 170697) used as the closest defensible analogue for waffle-cut deep-fried fries. Waffle fries have a similar potato-to-oil ratio to standard cut fries. Sodium adjusted upward by approximately 15% to account for surface seasoning powder typical of Potato Corner products. Energy, fat, and carbohydrate values are consistent with deep-fried potato products reported in USDA Foundation Foods and peer-reviewed literature on fried potato snacks. Values represent a best-estimate adaptation; exact Potato Corner formulation is not publicly available in an approved source.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; French fries / fried potato entries
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No GI value specific to waffle-cut fries was identified in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 75 (high) is taken from the published GI value for french fries / deep-fried potato products reported in Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care 31:2281–2283) and corroborated by the University of Sydney GI Database entry for french fries (GI ~75, glucose reference). Waffle-cut fries share the same base ingredient (potato) and cooking method (deep-frying), making this an appropriate analogous estimate. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (75 × 46.6) / 100 ≈ 34.9, where carbohydrate per 115 g serving = 40.5 × 1.15 = 46.6 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from seasoning powder (~380 mg per 100 g; ~437 mg per serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid seasoned waffle fries due to the high sodium load from flavouring powders, which can contribute significantly to the recommended daily sodium ceiling of 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic index (GI 75) and high glycemic load per serving (~34.9), driven by deep-fried starchy potato base
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should treat Mega Waffle Fries as an occasional food only, as the high GI and glycemic load can cause rapid postprandial blood glucose elevation; portion control and pairing with low-GI foods or protein is strongly advised.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate total fat (15 g/100 g) with low saturated fat (2.2 g/100 g) but elevated sodium and high caloric density
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: For individuals with cardiovascular disease, the elevated sodium content and high caloric density of seasoned waffle fries warrant portion restriction; the relatively low saturated fat content is a modest mitigating factor, but frequent consumption is not recommended.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Mega Waffle Fries are a large-portion serving of waffle-cut deep-fried potato fries sold by Potato Corner, a Filipino-origin fast-food chain with outlets across Southeast Asia including Singapore (e.g., Funan Mall). The fries are made from potatoes cut in a distinctive grid or waffle pattern using a crinkle-blade technique, then deep-fried in vegetable oil until golden and crispy. The waffle shape creates a larger surface area compared to standard straight-cut fries, resulting in a crispier exterior and a fluffy interior. They are served hot and tossed or dusted with one of Potato Corner's signature powdered seasonings, which range from classic cheese and barbecue to sour cream and spicy variants. The seasoning is a defining feature and contributes meaningfully to the sodium content. Nutritionally, waffle fries are a calorie-dense, high-carbohydrate snack (~312 kcal per 100 g, ~40.5 g carbohydrate per 100 g) with moderate fat (~15 g per 100 g) and modest protein (~3.5 g per 100 g). The glycemic index is high (~75), making them a concern for blood glucose management in individuals with Type 2 Diabetes or insulin resistance. The sodium content (~380 mg per 100 g) is elevated due to seasoning and is relevant for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. Dietary fibre (~2.8 g per 100 g) comes from the potato. The food is generally considered halal and vegetarian (depending on seasoning choice), though allergen status varies by flavour. Mega Waffle Fries are typically consumed as a snack or side dish and are popular among children and young adults in Singapore's food-court and mall dining culture.
common_user_queries: How many calories in Mega Waffle Fries?
Are Potato Corner waffle fries halal?
What is the GI of waffle fries?
Is Mega Waffle Fries suitable for diabetics?
How much sodium is in seasoned waffle fries?
Can I eat waffle fries if I have high blood pressure?

confidence_reason: No exact nutritional entry for Potato Corner Mega Waffle Fries exists in any approved primary source (HPB Singapore Foodo, USDA FoodData Central branded, ICMR-NIN). Values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy french fries data (FDC ID 170697) with a sodium adjustment for seasoning. GI is an analogous estimate from published french fries GI data. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy FDC ID 170697 (french fries, frozen, oven-heated) with sodium adjusted upward ~15% to reflect surface seasoning powder. Exact Potato Corner formulation is proprietary and not available in any approved source. 2. GI of 75 is an analogous estimate based on published deep-fried french fries GI data (Atkinson et al. 2008, Diabetes Care; University of Sydney GI Database). 3. The input record classified this food under food_group 'Desserts', which is incorrect; reclassified to 'Snacks' as waffle fries are a savoury fried potato snack. 4. Allergen profile is seasoning-dependent; the base fry is typically gluten-free but many Potato Corner seasonings contain milk, gluten, or soy. 5. vitamin_a_mcg_rae is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy source entry used. 6. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy source entry used. 7. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 as potatoes and vegetable oil contain no vitamin B12; confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central. 8. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 as the product is fried in vegetable oil with no animal-derived fat; confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central for comparable fried potato products. 9. Multilingual names provided for Singaporean context (Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi) as the food is sold in Singapore. 10. FoodOn and FoodEx2 codes are closest_parent matches; no exact waffle-fries ontology term exists in either system as of 2026-04.
